events.title: Open Field Trial Championship

events.title: Prairie Hills Novice Field Trial

events.title: Pre-Summer Cleanup & Workday

events.title: Advanced Field Handling Workshop

events.title: Advanced Field Handling Workshop – Water & Cover
events.description: Intensive two-day advanced workshop emphasizing water marks and heavy cover retrieves for experienced teams.

events.title: Elite Field Handling Intensive
events.description: Two-day elite-level workshop with small groups and individualized coaching. Designed for highly competitive teams.

events.title: Spring Kickoff Barbecue

events.title: Annual Membership Meeting

events.title: Kids Intro to Hunting Dogs Day

events.title: Intermediate Marking & Memory Workshop

events.title: Summer Retriever Field Trial

events.title: September Cleanup & Cookout Workday

articles.title: Helping Your Young Dog Overcome Gun Shyness
articles.summary: A stepwise beginner-friendly plan to rebuild confidence in dogs that have shown signs of gun shyness.
articles.body:
Gun shyness can develop quickly when a young dog has a bad first experience with loud noise. The good news is that most mild to moderate cases can be improved with a patient, structured plan.

In this article you will learn:
- How to recognize early signs of gun shyness
- Common mistakes that accidentally make the problem worse
- A step-by-step desensitizing plan using low-level background noise
- When to pause, when to progress, and when to get professional help

1. Start with environmental confidence
Before you work on noise, make sure your dog is relaxed in the training area. Use simple games, food rewards, and short retrieves far away from gunfire. Avoid any loud noises during this phase.

2. Pair low-level sound with something fun
Play recordings of distant gunfire or soft clapping at a very low volume while you hand-feed or play with your dog. If they show concern, turn the volume down and increase distance.

3. Gradually increase intensity
Over several short sessions, slowly increase either the volume or proximity of the sound source, but never both at once. Watch your dog closely. If they show stress (ears pinned, tail tucked, freezing), you have moved too fast.

4. Add retrieves and field-like context
Once your dog happily ignores low-level noise, add simple retrieves. Always start with the retrieve first, then add a soft sound while your dog is in motion or carrying the dummy. The retrieve should be the main event.

5. Introduce real gunfire carefully
Use a .22 blank or starter pistol at a long distance (100+ yards) while your dog is focused on a retrieve. Have a helper fire one shot as your dog is running toward the dummy. If the dog notices but resumes work, you can repeat at that distance for several sessions before moving closer.

6. Know when to stop
If your dog refuses to take food, tries to leave, or shuts down after noise, stop the session and go back several steps at the next training opportunity.

7. When to call a trainer
If your dog gets worse despite careful work, or if the shyness began after a very bad scare, consult a trainer experienced in gun-shy rehabilitation. Many clubs offer private evaluations and tailored plans.

Move slowly, keep sessions short and positive, and celebrate small wins. Gun shyness is easier to prevent than fix, but with patience many dogs can return to confident work in the field.

articles.title: Step-by-Step Plan to Prevent Gun Shyness in Puppies
articles.summary: A simple beginner plan for introducing noise and gunfire to your puppy so gun shyness never develops.
articles.body:
Preventing gun shyness is far easier than fixing it. By introducing sound and gunfire in a controlled, positive way, you can build a dog that associates loud noise with birds, retrieves, and fun.

1. Build a confident, birdy puppy first
Focus your early weeks on socialization, bird introduction, and play retrieves in quiet areas. Avoid fireworks displays and random gunfire until your puppy has strong positive associations with the field.

2. Add everyday household noise
Expose your puppy to clattering dishes, dropped books, and doors closing while you calmly ignore the sounds and reward relaxed behavior. The goal is not to scare the pup, but to normalize sudden but mild noises.

3. Use recorded sound correctly
If you use recordings of gunfire, start at very low volume while hand-feeding or playing. If the puppy startles and recovers, that’s acceptable; if they hide, you’re going too fast.

4. Pair distant gunfire with birds or dummies
Have a helper fire a starter pistol several hundred yards away while you let the pup chase a clipped-wing pigeon or run an easy dummy retrieve. The shot should be background noise, not the focus.

5. Move closer in small steps
Over several sessions, reduce the distance in 25–50 yard increments. Keep the puppy in drive with birds or fun retrieves before any shot is fired. Always stop on a good response.

6. Avoid common pitfalls
- Do NOT fire a shotgun over a pup’s head on the first day in the field.
- Do NOT combine first gunfire with other stressors like heavy cover or cold water.
- Do NOT push through if your pup is showing fear.

7. Keep sessions short and end on success
A couple of well-planned shots in each session is enough. Your puppy should leave wanting more.

With a methodical approach, most puppies can be introduced to gunfire with zero drama and will grow into confident, steady hunting partners.

articles.title: Noise Sensitivity vs. True Gun Shyness
articles.summary: Learn the difference between a generally noise-sensitive dog and a dog that is specifically gun-shy, and what to do in each case.
articles.body:
Not every dog that startles at loud sounds is truly gun-shy. Understanding the difference between general noise sensitivity and a specific gunfire problem helps you choose the right training plan.

This article covers:
- How to evaluate your dog’s response to everyday noise
- Patterns that suggest noise sensitivity
- Behaviors that point to gun-specific fear
- Adjusting your desensitization plan accordingly

We also include sample exercises for both categories and criteria for when to seek professional help.

articles.title: Checklist: Safe Gunfire Introduction for Young Dogs
articles.summary: A printable checklist to help you plan each step of your dog’s first gunfire experiences.
articles.body:
Use this checklist to plan and log your dog’s gunfire introduction. Each step includes recommended distance, noise level, and what behavior you should see before progressing.

Sections include:
- Pre-gunfire confidence checks
- Environmental and bird exposure
- Distant shots with birds or dummies
- Reducing distance in safe increments
- Troubleshooting notes for setbacks.

articles.title: Beginner Obedience for the Hunting Dog
articles.summary: Core obedience skills every hunting dog should know before their first season in the field.
articles.body:
Field obedience is about reliable, calm behavior around birds, guns, and other dogs. This article outlines the basic skills you should develop before you worry about advanced drills.

Core topics:
- Name response and recall
- Heel and loose-leash walking in distracting environments
- Place and stay around birds and other dogs
- Kennel and crate manners at the truck

We provide sample exercises you can work on at home and at the club training grounds, plus tips for when to join a beginner group class.

articles.title: Building Reliable Steadiness at the Line
articles.summary: Progressive drills to keep your dog steady around birds, shots, and other dogs.
articles.body: Steadiness at the line is the foundation of safe, polished field work. In this article we cover progressive exercises that develop impulse control without killing drive.

articles.title: Water Entry Confidence for Reluctant Swimmers
articles.summary: Gentle progressions to help hesitant dogs enjoy water retrieves.
articles.body: Some dogs charge into water; others need time. This guide shows you how to introduce water in a way that grows confidence without forcing your dog.

articles.title: Your First Novice Field Trial: What to Expect
articles.summary: A walkthrough of the entry process, running order, and common etiquette for novice field trials.
articles.body: Entering your first novice field trial can feel intimidating. This article explains how to read the premium, choose a suitable stake, complete the entry form, and prepare your dog for the running order.

articles.title: Designing a Two-Week Evening Training Plan
articles.summary: How to use the club schedule to build a short evening training plan on a budget.
articles.body: With busy workdays, evening sessions are the most realistic option for many handlers. This article shows you how to: use the online schedule, filter for after-6 pm drop-in sessions, balance obedience, water work, and steadiness, and track your total training costs.

articles.title: Choosing the Right Training Dummy for Your Dog
articles.summary: Canvas, rubber, foam birds, and bumpers—how to choose the right dummy for each stage of training.
articles.body: Training dummies come in many sizes and materials. In this guide we compare canvas, rubber, neoprene, and foam bird dummies, and explain when to use each. We also cover color choices, weight, and how to introduce scented dummies.

articles.title: Foundation Skills for Pointing Breeds
articles.summary: Key foundation work for pointing breeds, from recall and check cord work to early steadiness.
articles.body: Pointing dogs require a balance of independence and cooperation. This article outlines the foundational skills to build in the first year, including recall, quartering patterns, and early steadiness on birds.

articles.title: Club Membership Plans Explained
articles.summary: Compare the club’s membership options, including how many handlers are included and what grounds access you receive.
articles.body: This article explains each current membership plan, including the number of handlers allowed, training grounds access days, and typical payment schedules. It also offers examples of which plan fits common situations such as single handlers, couples who both train, and families with youth handlers.

articles.title: Preparing for a Volunteer Workday at the Training Grounds
articles.summary: What to bring, what to wear, and what to expect when you sign up for a club workday.
articles.body: Volunteer workdays keep our training grounds safe and enjoyable. This article describes typical tasks (brush clearing, equipment setup, mowing support), recommended clothing and gear, and how to indicate your task preferences when you sign up online.

articles.title: What to Expect in a Weekend Beginner Retriever Class
articles.summary: An overview of how our weekend beginner retriever obedience classes are structured and what skills you’ll work on.
articles.body: Weekend beginner retriever classes focus on basic obedience with a hunting-dog flavor. Sessions usually include heel work, sits and downs under distraction, intro to place boards, simple recalls, and short marked retrieves on dummies.

articles.title: FAQ: Common Questions About Gun Shyness
articles.summary: Answers to frequently asked questions about causes, prognosis, and training options for gun-shy dogs.
articles.body: This FAQ answers common questions such as: Is my dog ruined if it shows gun shyness? How long does rehab usually take? Can an older dog develop gun shyness later in life? When should I seek professional help? We include brief, practical answers and links to longer articles on prevention and rehabilitation.

reading_list_items.note: Use these steps with my 10-month-old pup
Good example of mixing obedience, water, and steadiness sessions.
Compare Household 7-Day vs Premium 2-Handler before applying.

training_programs.title: Weekend Beginner Retriever Obedience - April
training_programs.short_description: Four-week weekend morning class for young retrievers focusing on field-ready obedience basics.
training_programs.full_description:
This beginner-level weekend class is designed for young retrievers that need a solid obedience foundation with a hunting-dog focus. We work on recall, loose-leash heel, sit and stay around other dogs, intro to place boards, and short marked retrieves with dummies.

Handlers learn how to set clear expectations, use rewards effectively, and manage an excitable retriever in busy environments. Class meets on weekend mornings so working handlers can attend consistently.

training_programs.title: Weeknight Puppy Obedience Foundations - March
training_programs.short_description: A five-week evening class covering home and field obedience foundations for hunting-breed puppies.
training_programs.full_description:
This class is aimed at puppies 4–10 months old of any hunting breed. We cover name response, recall, leash manners, sit/down, short stays, and calm behavior in a group setting. Sessions include handling skills for the owner and simple games that build engagement.

This is an ideal first class before joining field-focused programs later in the year.

training_programs.title: Advanced Field Handling Series - Early Spring
training_programs.short_description: Four-week advanced series on multiple marks, cold blinds, and complex handling setups.
training_programs.full_description:
This advanced series is for dogs that already run confident single and simple double marks and have basic blind retrieve experience. We work on challenging marking concepts, cold blinds, diversions, and honoring other working dogs.

Handlers receive detailed coaching on reading the dog, using the whistle and hand signals, and planning training setups between sessions.

training_programs.title: Drop-In Water Retrieve Evenings - April
training_programs.short_description: Pay-per-session drop-in water retrieve practice for retrievers on Tuesday evenings.
training_programs.full_description:
These April drop-in sessions provide structured water retrieve practice for novice and experienced retrievers. Each evening includes warm-up drills, memory work, and simple handling exercises from the bank.

Handlers can attend any or all dates; each visit is paid per session. Dogs should be comfortable swimming and retrieving bumpers or dummies.

training_programs.title: Novice Steadiness on Birds - April Series
training_programs.short_description: Four-week evening series building sit/stay and steadiness around birds and other dogs.
training_programs.full_description:
This series focuses on developing reliable steadiness for novice dogs that already have basic obedience. We use place boards, remote sits, and controlled bird flushes to teach dogs to remain under control when excited.

Suitable for pointing, flushing, and retriever breeds preparing for their first season or first tests.

training_programs.title: Advanced Field Handling Workshop - March
training_programs.short_description: Two-day advanced field workshop on complex marks, blinds, and handling on rolling terrain.
training_programs.full_description:
This intensive two-day workshop is designed for advanced retriever teams competing in hunt tests or field trials. We train on rolling terrain with technical marking setups, cold blinds, and honoring scenarios.

Working spots are limited to 8 dogs to ensure individual coaching. Auditors are welcome to attend without dogs.

training_programs.title: Advanced Water & Cover Field Workshop - April
training_programs.short_description: Two-day advanced workshop emphasizing water marks and heavy cover retrieves.
training_programs.full_description:
This advanced workshop focuses on technical water and heavy cover work for experienced retrievers. Setups include crosswind water marks, re-entries, and mixed cover marking.

Participation is capped at 6 working dogs for even more individual attention. Handlers should be comfortable running at senior/finished or open-level concepts.

training_programs.title: Elite Field Handling Intensive - May
training_programs.short_description: High-level two-day intensive for serious field trial and hunt test competitors.
training_programs.full_description:
This intensive is aimed at handlers actively campaigning dogs at the highest levels. Setups are designed to challenge teamwork, control, and marking under pressure.

Due to the demanding nature of the work, this event is not suitable for novice or green dogs.

training_programs.title: Advanced Field Concepts Workshop - Memorial Weekend
training_programs.short_description: Two-day advanced workshop with a larger working group and mixed concept setups.
training_programs.full_description: A cost-effective advanced workshop with a slightly larger working group. We cover a wide mix of marking and blind concepts suitable for dogs preparing for senior/seasoned and early finished work.

training_programs.title: Drop-In Evening Field Obedience - March
training_programs.short_description: Flexible drop-in obedience sessions for hunting dogs after work.
training_programs.full_description:
These drop-in sessions focus on practical obedience for the hunting dog: recalls off distractions, heel work around other dogs, place/stay with light bird activity, and polite behavior at the truck.

Each evening stands alone, so you can attend whenever your schedule allows. Ideal for handlers building a short-term evening training plan.

training_programs.title: Drop-In Water Retrieve Evenings - March
training_programs.short_description: Evening water retrieve practice sessions for novice and intermediate dogs.
training_programs.full_description:
March water sessions are designed to ease dogs back into work after winter. We focus on short, confidence-building swims, simple marks, and basic lining from the bank.

Handlers pay per session and can mix these evenings into a two-week training plan alongside obedience and steadiness sessions.

training_programs.title: Drop-In Steadiness & Place Board Evenings - March
training_programs.short_description: After-work drop-in sessions to improve steadiness and place work.
training_programs.full_description:
These sessions emphasize impulse control: holding sit or place while other dogs work, staying steady for bumper throws, and maintaining position around light bird activity.

Great for building a budget-friendly three-session evening plan when combined with obedience and water retrieve drop-ins.

training_programs.title: Weekend Novice Pointing Dog Field Class - May
training_programs.short_description: Novice pointing dog class covering check cord work, quartering, and early steadiness.
training_programs.full_description:
This weekend class is geared toward novice pointing dogs that have basic obedience. We focus on check cord handling, quartering patterns in light cover, and early steadiness on planted birds.

The class runs for four Saturday mornings in May at the training grounds.

training_programs.title: 60-Minute Private Evaluation - Pointing Breeds
training_programs.short_description: One-on-one 60-minute evaluation session for pointing breed teams.
training_programs.full_description:
This private evaluation gives you a full hour with a pointing-breed specialist to assess your dog’s current level and outline a training plan. We look at obedience, search pattern, responsiveness on the check cord, and bird contact when available.

The evaluation can be scheduled on a weekday evening or weekend by arrangement with the trainer.

training_programs.title: Gun Shyness Prevention & Rehab Seminar
training_programs.short_description: Half-day seminar on preventing and addressing gun shyness in hunting dogs.
training_programs.full_description:
This classroom and field demo seminar covers how gun shyness develops, how to prevent it in puppies, and structured rehab plans for mildly gun-shy dogs. We review common mistakes, show sample desensitization setups, and leave plenty of time for questions.

Participants receive printed checklists and links to related club articles.

training_programs.title: Beginner Retriever Obedience - May Weeknights
training_programs.short_description: Evening beginner retriever class for handlers who prefer weekday training.
training_programs.full_description:
This class mirrors the weekend beginner retriever curriculum but meets on weeknight evenings. We focus on leash manners, recall, place training, and short marked retrieves using dummies.

Ideal for young retrievers or older dogs that are new to formal training.

training_sessions.title: Drop-In Water Retrieve Evening

training_sessions.title: Drop-In Water Retrieve Evening

training_sessions.title: Drop-In Steadiness & Place Board Evening

training_sessions.title: Drop-In Steadiness & Place Board Evening

training_sessions.title: Drop-In Steadiness & Place Board Evening
training_sessions.description: Mix of beginner and intermediate steadiness drills tailored to each dog.

training_sessions.description: First week of the April beginner retriever obedience class: introductions, leash skills, and recalls.

training_sessions.title: Weekend Beginner Retriever Class - Week 2

training_sessions.title: Advanced Field Handling Workshop - Day 2
